dialogue.utterance: Good afternoon, I'm Alex with EMS. What caused you to call 911?
My wife and I measured my blood sugar this afternoon, and it was 452. We called the doctor and they said to come in immediately.
Great. You seem alert - are you aware of who you are, where you are, and what time it is?
I'm Charles, we're in [CITY], and it's October.
You're alert to person, place, and time - which is a Glasgow Coma Scale of 15.
Pulse is 95, blood pressure is 165 over 83.
Respiratory rate is 22, no signs of respiratory distress.
Have you experienced any sweating, nausea, or shaking?
No, I felt okay after the blood test.
We've noted in your records: primary hypertension, back pain, and type 2 diabetes with insulin dependence.
You mentioned no pain - just to confirm, you're at a zero on the pain scale?
Yes, no pain at all.
Your respiratory rate is 22, with 96% oxygen saturation.
EKG confirms normal sinus rhythm (NSR) with no changes.
Blood glucose measurement is 426 mg/dL.
Later readings show 138 over 75 blood pressure during transport.
We are placing the 4-lead ECG monitor for your safety.
We are starting normal saline via IV in your arm to assist with hydration - it's a standing order in protocol 3-7.
IV placed in an extremity vein (arm or leg), 700 mL of normal saline running per protocol.
The 4-lead ECG continues to show no changes and maintains normal sinus rhythm.
Skin is smooth, warm, and without signs of distress. You appear fully alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
A later reading shows a respiratory rate of 27.
We're taking you to [COMPANY_NAME] for further evaluation on scene as per the doctor's advice.
Can you please lie down on the cot now to prepare for transport?
We'll monitor your breathing and heart rhythm the entire time.
Upon arrival - let the nurse know IV is active, initial glucose was 426, you're A&O x 4, and you have no visible distress (NAD).
We should note any known allergies, such as penicillin, to ensure safe treatment.
Your medical records indicate allergy to penicillin and aspirin.
We make sure to avoid all medications you're allergic to during treatment.
This treatment follows protocol 3-7 for medical-diabetic hyperglycemia as outlined.
The EKG confirms NSR throughout the encounter with no arrhythmias noted.
Your airway is patent and not obstructed; no sounds of obstruction noted.
Still sounds clear, so we'll maintain your current position for comfort.
We've secured the cot and will move you carefully for transport.
We've completed the full examination and now monitor for any changes.
No signs or symptoms of genitourinary distress reported.
Just to confirm, have you had anything to eat or drink before this?
I had a small sandwich about two hours ago.
No deformities or angulations observed in the extremities.
Neck veins are unremarkable; no swelling or nodules.
No obstruction or foreign body found in ears, nose, or throat.
Your pulse has stabilized at 86 during transport.
We will continue to document your vital signs every 5 minutes.
IV flow appears smooth, with proper arm positioning and IV site intact.
We'll check your responsiveness again as a routine part of care.
Your eyes open spontaneously, which is consistent with full alertness.
You respond appropriately to questions - a 6 on the motor scale.
Your skin turgor is normal; no signs of dehydration.
No signs of insect bites or stings observed during skin inspection.
I'll report all findings and interventions as required during transport.
A few small scars on the arms, but they're not relevant to today's episode.
Your chest rises and falls symmetrically with each breath.
Breath sounds are bilateral and consistent.
Peripheral and central pulses match; no discrepancies noted.
Your regular medications are noted: Glucotrol, Metformin, Toprol, among others.
You're not currently on any medications for this episode per your records.
Could you retell the sequence of events that led to the 911 call?
We checked my sugar, it was high, and the doctor said to come in right away.
You're able to follow instructions clearly, suggestive of intact short-term memory.
Mentally, you're alert, oriented, and showing good judgment.
We're ready for movement - the cot and straps are properly fastened.
What position is most comfortable for you during travel?
I can lie back fine; the cot supports me well.
You're doing the right thing by seeking evaluation today.
I'll provide full care en route and keep the nurse fully updated upon arrival.
Skin remains warm, with no signs of pallor, cyanosis, or jaundice.
I'll recheck your vitals in five and keep the readings updated for the hospital.
Your IV site looks clean and dry; let me adjust the position a bit.
Your breathing remains unlabored with no new discomfort reported.
Pulse slowing to 86 now.
No changes so far in vitals after the last check.
No signs of new onset distress or complications - you're doing well today.
Vitals from arrival were similar to most recent.
To recap for transport logs: no allergies to current meds, no new symptoms.
You are still pain-free on a zero scale and not short of breath.
Just rechecking BP again-still 138/75.
You're en route safely; we're keeping track of all vitals and IV.
No new findings on the thoracic or lung assessments.
We're doing fast repeats for now due to protocol.
Skin inspection and palpation are normal as well.
Oxygen saturation is still stable at 96 percent.
No signs of distension or tenderness in the belly.
No signs of discoloration or other concerning features on limbs.
You're still alert, with no signs of a neurological event.
Straight ahead, prepared for hospital arrival.
The transport site is clean, cot is safe for scene, and we are on schedule.
I've updated the dispatch and hospital with the current status.
Patient is a 73-year-old male with known diabetes, BP elevated initially but stabilized.
Glucose entering ER at 426 mg/dL; IV fluids started and running stable.
EKG showed NSR throughout.
Allergies present include penicillin and aspirin, no acute reaction observed.
Please note patient has a known penicillin and aspirin allergy.
Oral cavity, ears, and nose are clear and functional.
The cot is reclined to promote comfort during arrival.
All medications, interventions, and reasons clearly reviewed for the nurse.
Your back does not appear to be in any acute distress.
Gentle repositioning confirms no signs of pelvic instability.
One final check for EKG and respiration shows no new developments.
No new interventions needed, just steady IV fluids and monitoring.
Readings and intake are fully documented in the report for the ER team.
Last oral intake was about two hours ago with light food.
No changes in airway patency or comfort expressed.
Did not notice any irregularities in breathing during the entire time.
Blood pressure and pulse are steady; no new concerns about circulation.
All vitals are stable as we finish transport and arrive at the facility.
All documentation and summaries are in the EPCR as well.
The IV is secure and still running, blood sugar steady.
We just parked - ready for your nurse to take over.
Cot is in a comfortable setting for transitioning to the bed.
Report has been transmitted with all findings as noted in the ground truth.
The vital readings and protocols performed are accurate as per the ePCR.
Your notes, physically and digitally, are complete and signed.
Thanks, Charles, and we're glad we could help you get to the ER safely.
